article:
Chunk # 0.
ANALYTICAL BIOCHEMISTRY 93, 200--203 (1979) 
A Rapid Method for the Isolation of Intact Mitochondria 
from Isolated Rat Liver Cells 
P,g,R GELLERFORS AND B DEAN NELSON 
Department of Biochemistry, Arrhenius Laboratory, University of Stockholm, S-106 91 Stockholm, Sweden 
Received June 7, 1978 
A method is described for the preparation of intact mitochondria from isolated hepatocytes 
by sonication Sonication of a suspension of rat liver cells for 10-30 s yields a homogenate 
from which tightly coupled mitochondria can be isolated These mitochondria exhibit high 
respiratory control ratios and normal ADP:O ratios using glutamate plus malate, fl-hydroxy- 
butyrate, succinate, or ascorbate plus N,N,N',N'-tetramethyl-p-phenylendiamine as 
substrates The yield of mitochondrial protein is approximately 100-120 mg starting from 5 g 
of liver tissue The mitochondrial fraction is essentially free of contaminating plasma 
membrane and microsomes and contains only small amounts of peroxisomes and lysosomes 
When hepatocytes are isolated from rat 
liver tissue the plasma membrane becomes 
resistant to mechanical breakage The usual 
method of disruption by homogenization 
becomes ineffective, and difficulty is experi- 
enced in preparing intact subcellular organ- 
elles Zuurendonk and Tager (1) reported 
the use of digitonin to aid in the breakage 
of the plasma membrane of isolated hepa- 
tocytes This method has been used for the 
rapid separation of cellular particles from 
the soluble fraction without concern for the 
effects of digitonin on the structure and 
function of the organelles To our knowledge 
no other method for the effective release of 
subcellular particles from isolated hepato- 
cytes has been reported 
The present paper describes a new and 
rapid method for disruption of the plasma 
membrane from isolated hepatocytes by 
sonication This method is developed in 
order to isolate highly coupled mitochondria 
and is thus also suitable for the prepara- 
tion of other subcellular organelles from 
hepatocytes 
MATERIALS AND METHODS 
Materials Collagenase from Clostridium 
histolyticum (Grade II) was purchased from 
0003-2697/79/030200-04502.00/0 
Copyright © 1979 by Academic Press Inc 
All rights of reproduction in any form reserved 200 Boehringer Trypan blue (Sigma) was made 
as a 0.6% (w/v) solution in 0.9% NaC1 and 
stored frozen The nylon filters used to filter 
hepatocytes, PE 105 HD Super (mesh count 
per inch: 101.6) and PE 40 HD Super (mesh 
count per inch: 246.1), were purchased from 
Tidbecks, Stockholm, Sweden All other 
chemicals used were reagent grade 
The buffers used were the same as de- 
scribed by Seglen (2,3) The Ca2+-free buffer 
contained 142 mM NaC1, 6.7 mM KC1, and 
10 mM Hepes, 1 pH 7.4 The collagenase 
buffer contained 66.7 mM NaC1, 6.7 mM 
KC1, 4.8 mM CaC12, 100 mM Hepes, and 
0.50 mg/ml collagenase, pH 7.6 The suspen- 
sion buffer contained 68 mM NaC1, 5.4 mM 
KC1, 1.2 mM CaC12, 0.6 mM MgC12, 1.1 mM 
KH2PO4, 0.7 mM Na2SO4, 30 mM Hepes, 
30 mM Tes, and 36 mM Tricine, pH 7.6 
The pH of the perfusion buffers was ad- 
justed with NaOH 
Procedure Rat liver cells were prepared 
as described by Seglen (2,3) except that the 
rat (180 g) was anesthetized by the intra- 
1 Abbreviations used: Hepes, N-2-hydroxyethyl- 
piperazine-N'-2-ethanesulfonic acid; Tes, N-tris 
[hydroxymethyl]methyl-2-aminoethanesulfonic acid; 
Tricine, N-tris(hydroxymethyl)methylglycine; FCCP, 
carbonylcyanide-p-trifluoromethoxyphenylhydrazone 
MITOCHONDRIA FROM ISOLATED HEPATOCYTES 201 
peritoneal administration of 0.35 ml of 
Nembutal (50 mg/ml) and that 0.1 ml of 
heparin (5000 IU/ml) was injected intra- 
venously before the liver was perfused 
Approximately 280 ml of the Ca2+-free buffer 
and 50 ml of the collagenase buffer were 
perfused at a rate of 40 ml/min for 7 and 
8 min, respectively, the collagenase buffer 
being recirculated All perfusion buffers 
used were oxygenated at 37°C The perfused 
liver was suspended in 150 ml of cold sus- 
pension buffer and the cells were liberated 
and filtered through a nylon filter (PE 105 
HD Super) and washed four times with 
150 ml of the same buffer by centrifugation 
at 50g for 2.5 min After the final washing the 
hepatocytes were again filtered through two 
nylon filters (PE 105 HD Super and PE 40 
HD Super) The hepatocytes were 90% viable 
as determined by trypan blue exclusion 
Hepatocytes used for the isolation of 
mitochondria were suspended in cold 0.25 M 
sucrose to a protein concentration of 
10 mg/ml (300 x 104 cells/ml) Aliquots 
(20 ml) of the cell suspension were placed 
in plastic Servall centrifuge tubes (28 x 101 
mm) and sonicated with a Branson sonifier 
(Model S-125) for 5 to 60 s at the lowest 
output intensity The sonication was done 
with a microtip of approximately 5 mm 
diameter, which was immersed approxi- 
mately 10 mm into the cell suspension The

mitochondria.protocol:
**Mitochondrial Isolation Steps:**

1. Sonication of a suspension of rat liver cells for 10-30 s yields a homogenate.
2. Tightly coupled mitochondria can be isolated from the homogenate.
3. The yield of mitochondrial protein is approximately 100-120 mg starting from 5 g of liver tissue.
4. The mitochondrial fraction is essentially free of contaminating plasma membrane and microsomes and contains only small amounts of peroxisomes and lysosomes.
5. Hepatocytes are isolated from rat liver tissue using collagenase from Clostridium histolyticum.
6. The plasma membrane of isolated hepatocytes is disrupted by sonication.
7. The sonication method is suitable for the isolation of highly coupled mitochondria and other subcellular organelles from hepatocytes.

**Incompleteness or Uncertainties:**
- The specific duration of sonication is not provided (e.g., 10-30 s, but no exact time is mentioned).
- The concentration of collagenase used is not mentioned.
- The specific method for liberating and filtering the cells through nylon filters is not described in detail.
- The specific method for determining the viability of hepatocytes is not mentioned.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a detailed method for the isolation of intact mitochondria from isolated rat liver cells. The steps mentioned include sonication of liver cell suspension, isolation of tightly coupled mitochondria, and the absence of contaminating plasma membrane and microsomes. However, there are some protocol gaps and uncertainties regarding the duration of sonication, collagenase concentration, cell liberation and filtering method, and viability determination of hepatocytes.
mitochondria.organisms:
Organisms Mentioned in the Mitochondrial Extraction Protocol:

- Rat (Rattus norvegicus)
- Clostridium histolyticum (used for collagenase)

summary: Summary 4: A rapid method for isolating intact mitochondria from isolated rat liver cells is described in the article "A Rapid Method for the Isolation of Intact Mitochondria from Isolated Rat Liver Cells" by P.G.R. Gellerfors and B. Dean Nelson. The method involves sonication of a suspension of rat liver cells for 10-30 seconds to yield a homogenate from which tightly coupled mitochondria can be isolated. The yield of mitochondrial protein is approximately 100-120 mg starting from 5 g of liver tissue. The method ensures the mitochondrial fraction is essentially free of contaminating plasma membrane and microsomes and contains only small amounts of peroxisomes and lysosomes. The study was conducted at the Department of Biochemistry, Arrhenius Laboratory, University of Stockholm, S-106 91 Stockholm, Sweden. The method utilizes collagenase from Boehringer for the breakdown of collagen and Trypan blue from Sigma for cell viability testing. Nylon filters from Tidbecks are used for filtration purposes. The buffers used in the study were described by Seglen. The isolation of intact mitochondria is crucial for studying various metabolic pathways involving substrates such as glutamate, malate, fl-hydroxy-butyrate, succinate, and ascorbate plus N,N,N',N'-tetramethyl-p-phenylendiamine. The respiratory control ratios and ADP:O ratios of the isolated mitochondria were measured to assess their efficiency. The method is effective in obtaining highly coupled mitochondria and can be adapted for the isolation of other subcellular organelles from hepatocytes. The article also presents data on protein yields and respiratory control ratios of mitochondria prepared from isolated hepatocytes. References include Zuurendonk and Tager (1) and Seglen (2).

sonication procedure is approximately 100- 
120 mg from a 5-g liver, which is similar to 
that routinely obtained in our laboratory after 
homogenization of 5 g of liver tissue with a 
Potter-Elvehjem homogenizer (4,10) Thus, 
the method is rapid and the yields are as 
good as those obtained from intact liver 
tissue 
Mitochondria isolated from hepatocytes 
after 10-30 s of sonication are tightly 
coupled as shown by high respiratory control 
ratios (Figs 1, 2) and ADP:O ratios (Fig 2) 
Similar results were obtained with /3- 
hydroxybutyrate or succinate as substrates 
Little variation in the degree of intactness 
is seen with time of sonication (Fig 1) This 
is advantageous since the output intensities 
of different sonicators can vary As ex- 
pected, the mitochondria isolated after 
sonication are also sensitive to FCCP, rote- 
none, antimycin, cyanide, and oligomycin 
The purity of the mitochondrial fraction 
obtained after 30 s sonication is shown in 
Table 1 The table compares the distribution 
of various marker enzymes in the mitochon- 
drial pellet (4300g pellet) and the postmito- 
chondrial supernatant The mitochondrial 
fraction is virtually free of plasma membrane 
(5'-AMP phosphatase) and microsomes 
(rotenone insensitive NADPH-cytochrome c reductase) and contains approximately 
27% of the lysosomal (acid phosphatase) 
and peroxisomal (urate oxidase) fractions 
The latter two organelles are difficult to 
separate from mitochondria because of their 
similar densities (10), and more elaborate 
separation techniques must be used for com- 
plete separation 
CONCLUSION 
The sonication procedure described above 
provides a rapid and simple method for the 
preparation of mitochondria from isolated 
rat liver cells.The yield and purity of the 
mitochondria are equal to those obtained by 
homogenizing intact liver tissue Because of 
the high degree of intactness of the mito- 
chondria, the method appears to be very 
gentle and can thus be applied to the isolation 
of other subcellular components from iso- 
lated hepatocytes An additional advantage 
of the procedure is that hepatocytes incu- 
bated with radioactive material do not need 
to be homogenized, thus minimizing the 
handling of the radioactive material 
ACKNOWLEDGMENTS 
We would like to thank Barbro Persson for technical 
assistance

mitochondria.protocol:
1. Mitochondria were isolated from 200 mg of isolated rat liver cells.
2. The cells were sonicated for 10-30 seconds to disrupt them.
3. The disrupted cells were removed by centrifugation at 480g for 10 minutes.
4. The resulting supernatant was centrifuged at 4300g for 20 minutes to isolate the mitochondria.
5. The pellet containing the mitochondria was washed twice in 20 ml of 0.25 M sucrose at 4300g for 15 minutes.
6. The purity of the mitochondrial preparation was determined by assaying several marker enzymes, including rotenone insensitive NADPH-cytochrome c reductase, urate oxidase, acid phosphatase, Y-AMP phosphatase, cytochrome oxidase, and monoamine oxidase.
7. The yield of mitochondrial protein obtained by the sonication procedure was approximately 100-120 mg from a 5-g liver.
8. Mitochondria isolated after 10-30 seconds of sonication showed high respiratory control ratios and ADP:O ratios.
9. The mitochondria isolated after sonication were sensitive to FCCP, rotenone, antimycin, cyanide, and oligomycin.
10. The mitochondrial fraction obtained after 30 seconds of sonication was virtually free of plasma membrane and microsomes, and contained approximately 27% of the lysosomal and peroxisomal fractions.
11. The sonication procedure described is a rapid and simple method for the preparation of mitochondria from isolated rat liver cells, with a high degree of intactness.
12. The yield and purity of the mitochondria obtained by sonication are comparable to those obtained by homogenization of intact liver tissue.
13. The method can potentially be applied to the isolation of other subcellular components from isolated hepatocytes.
14. The procedure minimizes the handling of radioactive material when incubating hepatocytes with radioactive material.

**Conclusion:** The article provides a detailed protocol for the isolation of mitochondria from isolated rat liver cells using sonication. The protocol includes steps for cell disruption, removal of disrupted cells, centrifugation to isolate mitochondria, washing of the mitochondrial pellet, and assessment of mitochondrial purity. However, some details are missing, such as the specific sonication conditions (e.g., power, frequency), the composition of the washing solution, and the specific marker enzyme assays used for purity determination.

summary: Mitochondria were isolated from rat liver cells and used in an experiment to measure the rate of oxygen consumption. Glutamate and malate were used as substrates, and the respiratory control (RC) and ADP:O ratios were determined. The mitochondrial fraction was obtained by sonication and showed high respiratory control and ADP:O ratios. Marker enzyme assays confirmed the purity of the mitochondrial preparation. The sonication method provides a rapid and gentle way to isolate mitochondria from liver cells. This procedure can be applied to isolate other subcellular components from hepatocytes.

summary:
Revised Summary: This work focuses on studying mitochondria from isolated hepatocytes using methods of enzymology and cell biology. The research is supported by grants from the Swedish Natural Sciences Research Council. Mitochondria play a crucial role in cellular function and energy production. Isolated hepatocytes are used in mitochondrial research to study their specific functions. Several researchers, including Zuurendonk, Tager, Seglen, Sottocasa, Kuylenstierna, Ernster, Bergstrand, Leighton, Poole, Lazarow, de Duve, Trover, Aronson, Touster, Wharton, Griffiths, Tabor, Rosenthal, Potter, and Elvehjem, have contributed to the field of mitochondrial research. Key references in mitochondrial research include "Biochim Biophys Acta," "Methods Cell Biol," "Exp Cell Res," "J Cell Biol," "Arch Biochem Biophys," and "J Biol Chem." Methods of Enzymology, edited by Fleischer, is a valuable resource in the study of mitochondria. Isolation of hepatocytes is an essential technique in mitochondrial research. The field of mitochondrial research encompasses biochemistry, biophysics, cell biology, and enzymology.

Missing Entities: Mitochondrial research in biochemistry and biophysics, specifically focusing on mitochondria from isolated hepatocytes, has been supported by grants from the Swedish Natural Sciences Research Council. This research has been published in various journals including Biochim Biophys Acta, Methods Cell Biol, Exp Cell Res, J Cell Biol, Arch Biochem Biophys, and J Biol Chem, as well as the book "Methods of Enzymology" by Fleischer.

topics.topic: Mitochondrial research in biochemistry and biophysics
topics.justification: The summary mentions that the work focuses on studying mitochondria from isolated hepatocytes using methods of enzymology and cell biology. This indicates a specific focus on mitochondrial research in the fields of biochemistry and biophysics.
